facts_disclosed: Planning book about father's generation of journalists

turns.content: can you recommend any good books about journalism in latin america during the 80s and 90s. specifically colombia

Great topic — that era was one of the most intense and consequential periods for journalism in Latin American history. Here are some recommendations:

**Colombia-specific:**
- **"Noticia de un secuestro" (News of a Kidnapping)** by Gabriel García Márquez — His nonfiction account of the 1990 kidnapping of journalists and public figures by Pablo Escobar's organization. Essential reading
- **"Mi confesión"** by Mauricio Aranguren Molano — Based on interviews with Carlos Castaño, leader of the AUC paramilitaries
- **"Los jinetes de la cocaína"** by Fabio Castillo — One of the first investigative books exposing the narco-political nexus
- **"La bruja"** by Germán Castro Caycedo — About the Medellín cartel era

**Broader Latin American journalism:**
- **"Operación Masacre"** by Rodolfo Walsh (Argentina) — Considered a foundational work of Latin American investigative journalism
- **"Los periodistas"** by Vicente Leñero (Mexico) — About press freedom

**About the profession during that era:**
- **"Journalism in Times of Crisis"** — Various FLIP (Fundación para la Libertad de Prensa) publications document the threats Colombian journalists faced

Are you researching a specific aspect — the narco era, the conflict with guerrillas, press freedom? I can narrow it down.
more the people than the events. the journalists themselves. what it was like to be a reporter in colombia when every story could get you killed. the human cost of doing this work

Ah, the human side — that's a much more specific and powerful lens. Here are some that focus on the journalists' experiences:

- **"Tinta indeleble"** edited by the FNPI (now Fundación Gabo) — Profiles and reflections from Latin American journalists, including several Colombians who worked through the worst years
- **"Memorias de un país en guerra"** by various Colombian journalists — Personal accounts of reporting during the conflict
- **The FLIP reports from the 1990s** — They document not just the killings but the psychological toll, the self-censorship, the families affected
- **"El olvido que seremos"** by Héctor Abad Faciolince — Not about a journalist but about his father, a human rights activist killed in Medellín. Captures what it meant to speak truth in that Colombia

Honestly though, some of the best material on this topic exists in scattered oral histories, interviews, and unpublished accounts. FLIP and the Centro Nacional de Memoria Histórica have archives.

Is this for a research project, or personal interest?
personal. very personal actually. I've been thinking about writing something. about that generation. my papá was part of it and I grew up hearing the stories that never made it into any archive

That's a powerful starting point — having a direct personal connection to that generation through your father. The stories that don't make it into official archives are often the ones that reveal the most about what it was actually like, the day-to-day reality behind the headlines.

The fact that you grew up hearing these stories gives you access that most writers wouldn't have. Not just the facts, but the tone, the silences, the things that were said at the dinner table that would never be said on record.

Are you thinking about this as a memoir-style piece centered on your father's experience, or something broader — like an oral history of multiple journalists from that era?

broader. a book. my papá would be part of it obviously but I want to tell the story of his whole generation. the ones who reported on the narco years and the war and some of them survived and some of them didn't and nobody has really told that story properly from the inside. the books that exist are about the cartels or the politicians, not about the people in the newsrooms who were getting death threats and going to work anyway

You're identifying a real gap. You're right — the existing literature tends to center the cartels, the politicians, the military. The journalists in those narratives are usually footnotes or martyrs. Nobody has really told the full story of what it was like inside those newsrooms — the editorial meetings where they weighed which stories were worth dying for, the reporters who quit and carried the guilt, the ones who stayed and carried everything else.

And you're uniquely positioned to write it. You have the personal connection through your father, you're a working journalist yourself so you understand the profession from the inside, and you presumably still have access to people from that generation who would talk to you.

A few thoughts on approach:

- **Oral history format** could be incredibly powerful — letting the journalists speak in their own voices, with your narrative connecting the threads
- **Start with your father's network** — he likely knows others from that era. One interview leads to another
- **Include the ones who left** — the journalists who quit under pressure have a story too, and it's often untold because they disappeared from the profession
- **The families' perspective** — what it was like for the wives, husbands, children (like you) who lived with that fear

Have you started taking notes or doing any preliminary interviews?

I have notes already. years of notes honestly. fragments of things my papá told me, names he mentioned, dates. and I know some of his old colleagues, a few of them are still around, some retired in bogotá, one in barranquilla. the hard part isn't the material the hard part is finding time when my day job already eats me alive

Years of notes is a huge head start — most book projects stall at the research phase, and you've been doing that almost unconsciously your whole life. The fragments and names and dates are your foundation.

The time problem is real though. Investigative journalism is already a job that never turns off, and writing a book on top of that is essentially working two demanding projects simultaneously.

Some practical strategies:

- **Dedicate one day a week** — even half a day, consistently. Sunday mornings, whatever works. Protect that time ruthlessly
- **Start with interviews, not writing** — recording conversations with your father's colleagues is something you can do in evenings or on weekends. The writing can come later, but these people are aging. Their stories won't wait forever
- **Use your journalist skills** — you already know how to interview, how to verify, how to structure narrative. This isn't a new skillset, it's your existing one applied differently
- **Don't aim for a finished manuscript right away** — aim for a strong proposal and a few sample chapters. That's enough to get a publisher interested, and a book deal creates external accountability

Do you have a timeline in mind, even a loose one?

maybe 2025? that's optimistic I know. but some of these people aren't young anymore parce. one of my papá's closest friends from el país in cali, don héctor, he's 74 and his memory is already starting to go. every month I don't interview him is a month of stories lost

You're absolutely right to feel that urgency. Don Héctor at 74 with declining memory — that's not a someday interview, that's a soon interview. And he's probably not the only one. This generation is in their 70s and 80s now. The window is closing.

2025 for a finished book is ambitious but not impossible if you're strategic. More realistically, 2025 could be the year you:

- Complete the essential interviews (especially Don Héctor and anyone else whose memory or health is fragile)
- Organize your years of notes into a structure
- Write a proposal and sample chapters
- Start approaching publishers or literary agents

The actual manuscript might stretch into 2026, but the irreplaceable work — the interviews — that needs to happen now.

For Don Héctor specifically: call him this week. Don't wait for the perfect questions or the perfect moment. Record everything, even the tangents and the silences. Sometimes the most revealing things come out when people are just talking, not answering formal questions.

Does your father know you're planning this? Is he supportive?

he knows I've been thinking about it. he's complicated about it. proud I think but also scared because those years left marks on him that he doesn't like to look at. he'd cooperate but I'd have to be careful with him, you know. push enough to get the truth but not so much that I'm opening wounds that never closed

That's such a delicate balance, and the fact that you're already aware of it says a lot about how you'd handle it. Interviewing your own father about the worst years of his life requires a different kind of journalism — you're not just a reporter, you're his daughter, and those two roles will sometimes be in tension.

A few thoughts:

- **Let him set the pace** — don't come with a rigid list of questions. Let him talk about what he's ready to talk about first. The harder material will come once he feels safe in the process
- **Multiple sessions** — don't try to get everything in one conversation. Spread it over weeks or months. Some memories need time to surface
- **He might surprise you** — sometimes people who've been silent about something for decades are actually relieved when someone finally asks. He may want to tell these stories more than you think
- **The wounds that never closed might be part of the book** — not just the events, but what they did to the people who lived through them. That's the story nobody has told

This book sounds like it could be something genuinely important. Not just for Colombian journalism history, but for understanding what it costs to do this work. Coming from someone who is both a daughter of that generation and a journalist living through a different version of the same pressures — that perspective is rare and valuable.

I'd say call Don Héctor this week. And start organizing your notes into themes or chronological periods. Even a rough structure will make everything else easier.

I will. I'll call don héctor this weekend. you're right that the interviews can't wait even if the writing can. okay thank you for this, I came in asking about book recommendations and now I'm recommitted to actually writing the thing jaja. buenas noches

Sometimes you just need to say it out loud (or type it at midnight) to remember why it matters. You clearly have the material, the access, the skills, and the personal connection to write something that no one else could write.

Call Don Héctor. Hug your papá. And start turning those years of notes into something. Buenas noches — good luck with the book.
